paragraphs: Polarized light, characterized by waves vibrating in a single plane, offers applications beyond its familiar use in sunglasses, where it reduces glare from reflective surfaces like water and roads. In certain scientific fields, it is invaluable for revealing hidden properties of materials. For instance, when researchers study the internal structure of crystals, polarized light provides insights that normal light cannot, by interacting differently with the distinct optical paths within the crystal lattice.

In biology, polarized light uncovers structural details of cells and tissues, facilitating breakthroughs in understanding cellular organization and disease mechanisms. This is true for birefringent materials, which alter the polarization of light in ways that reveal microstructural patterns such as fiber alignment and intracellular organization, helping scientists assess variations in cell density and composition. Such techniques have been instrumental in identifying amyloid plaques in brain tissue, an indication of Alzheimer's disease.

However, the use of polarized light is not universally beneficial. In aviation, pilots may avoid polarized lenses because they can obscure critical visual cues, such as instrument readings, that are essential for navigation. Thus, while polarized light can solve certain visibility issues by reducing glare, it may also interfere with necessary visual information, prompting careful consideration of context before implementation.

questions.question: The word "invaluable" in the passage is closest in meaning to

options.A: recommended
options.B: convenient
options.C: extremely useful
options.D: very expensive

questions.special_content: In certain scientific fields, it is <mark>invaluable</mark> for revealing hidden properties of materials.

questions.question: All of the following are mentioned as applications of polarized light EXCEPT:

options.A: It is used in sunglasses to reduce glare.
options.B: It can reveal hidden properties in crystals.
options.C: It can be used to alter cell structure.
options.D: It aids in analyzing cellular composition.

questions.question: What can be inferred about the use of polarized light in medicine?

options.A: It can help doctors diagnose diseases.
options.B: It is still in its early stages of implementation.
options.C: It offers new ways to treat Alzheimer's disease.
options.D: Its use is somewhat controversial.

options.A: give pilots a false sense of confidence
options.B: increase glare in the cockpit
options.C: become foggy at high altitudes
options.D: make it difficult to see aircraft instruments

options.D: Location 4

questions.special_content: [■] In biology, polarized light uncovers structural details of cells and tissues, facilitating breakthroughs in understanding cellular organization and disease mechanisms. [■] This is true for birefringent materials, which alter the polarization of light in ways that reveal microstructural patterns such as fiber alignment and intracellular organization, helping scientists assess variations in cell density and composition. [■] Such techniques have been instrumental in identifying amyloid plaques in brain tissue, an indication of Alzheimer's disease. [■]
questions.inserted_sentence: Applications are not limited to the inorganic world.

paragraphs: In 1985, the J. Paul Getty Museum acquired what it believed to be a Greek kouros (a statue of a naked youth) dating from the Archaic period (650–480 B.C.E.), estimating the date of origin as approximately 530 B.C.E. The Getty Kouros's authenticity soon came into question, however. Supporters cited scientific analyses suggesting that the statue's marble is consistent with marble known to have come from ancient Greek quarries and that its surface shows chemical changes consistent with long-term burial. Some scholars also argue that peculiar inconsistencies in the statue's style—it incorporates features from different regions and periods—could reflect a transitional moment in Greek sculpture.

Skeptics contend that these inconsistencies undermine the case for authenticity. The Kouros combines traits from several other known sixth-century B.C.E. kouroi in a way that appears too eclectic to be genuine. To these observers, the statue looks like a modern forger's creation—a confused imitation of Greek Archaic elements rather than the genuine product of a coherent sculptural tradition. Furthermore, scientists recognize that modern forgers can artificially age marble.
Because neither scientific analysis nor stylistic evaluation yields decisive results, the question of the Kouros's authenticity remains unresolved.

questions.question: What does the passage indicate about the "chemical changes" in the marble of the Getty Kouros?

options.A: They could not have occurred naturally.
options.B: They were caused by scientists testing the marble's age.
options.C: They explain some inconsistencies in features of the Getty Kouros.
options.D: They may suggest an ancient origin for the marble of the Getty Kouros.

questions.special_content: Supporters cited scientific analyses suggesting that the statue's marble is consistent with marble known to have come from ancient Greek quarries and that its surface shows <mark>chemical changes</mark> consistent with long-term burial.

questions.question: The author mentions "a transitional moment in Greek sculpture" primarily in order to

options.A: question a claim about sixth-century B.C.E. kouroi
options.B: point out how some people explain the style of the Getty Kouros
options.C: characterize sculptures typical of the Greek Archaic period
options.D: support the view that artistic styles evolve over time

questions.special_content: Some scholars also argue that peculiar inconsistencies in the statue's style—it incorporates features from different regions and periods—could reflect <mark>a transitional moment in Greek sculpture</mark>.

questions.question: What is the main reason that the authenticity of the Getty Kouros is in doubt?

options.A: The Greeks did not create many sculptures during the sixth century B.C.E.
options.B: Modern forgers are known to have created similar sculptures.
options.C: Observers may have misunderstood Greek Archaic elements.
options.D: The Getty Kouros combines styles that are too diverse.

questions.question: According to the passage, what is known about marble?

options.A: It can be quickly produced by forgers in large amounts.
options.B: It is difficult for forgers to imitate.
options.C: It can be made to appear older than it actually is.
options.D: Its traits could affect the style of Greek sculptures.

questions.question: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

options.A: The Getty Kouros is likely genuine, but scientific testing is needed to confirm its age.
options.B: The Getty Kouros is composed of marble that has probably been artificially aged.
options.C: The Getty Kouros is probably of Greek origin but unlikely to be from the Archaic period.
options.D: Whether the Getty Kouros is authentic is still a matter of debate among scientists and art historians.
